primary_diagnosis.name: Grade 1 lateral ankle sprain (anterior talofibular ligament)

pathophysiology_summary: An inversion (supination) force at the ankle stretches the lateral collateral ligament complex, of which the anterior talofibular ligament (ATFL) is the weakest and first injured. In a Grade 1 sprain the ligament fibres are overstretched with microscopic tearing but no macroscopic rupture and no mechanical instability, producing localised lateral pain, minimal swelling and preserved weight-bearing. Because there is no loss of joint integrity, the mainstay is protected early mobilisation and analgesia rather than immobilisation; rigid immobilisation delays recovery of proprioception and ligament remodelling. The principal task in this presentation is to distinguish a benign Grade 1 sprain from an occult malleolar or midfoot fracture — the Ottawa Ankle Rules provide a validated clinical decision aid for this, and much of the rule (ability to bear weight for four steps; tenderness over defined bony landmarks) is patient-reportable or patient-palpable under guidance.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic inversion mechanism with mild localised lateral pain and preserved weight-bearing.
Must be excluded after any inversion injury; Ottawa Ankle Rules stratify the need for imaging.
Common avulsion injury with the same inversion mechanism; midfoot component of the Ottawa rules.
Considered but less likely — typically caused by external rotation/dorsiflexion rather than pure inversion.
Posterior mechanism/pain pattern; not suggested by anterolateral pain with intact plantarflexion.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Early on, the correct posture is to keep fracture on the list and use a validated rule to exclude it, not to anchor on 'just a sprain'.

differential.evidence_basis: Weight-bearing preserved (four steps), no bony point tenderness at the malleoli, base of fifth metatarsal or navicular, no neurovascular symptoms — Ottawa Ankle Rules negative, fracture clinically unlikely.
Rendered unlikely by a negative Ottawa screen, but cannot be fully excluded via telehealth without imaging if any bony tenderness or inability to weight-bear emerges.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The negatives (weight-bearing, no bony tenderness) are what down-weight fracture — capture them explicitly rather than assuming.

differential.evidence_basis: Stable, minor ligamentous injury with preserved function; conservative management is appropriate, with imaging reserved for failure to improve or emergence of a red flag.

red_flags.description: Inability to bear any weight (cannot take four steps), or bony point tenderness over the posterior edge/tip of either malleolus, the base of the fifth metatarsal, or the navicular
red_flags.clinical_significance: These are the Ottawa Ankle Rules criteria; if present they mandate radiography to exclude fracture.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An occult malleolar or midfoot fracture is mobilised, risking displacement and delayed union.

red_flags.description: Neurovascular compromise of the foot — numbness, a cold or pale foot, or loss of pulses
red_flags.clinical_significance: Suggests vascular injury or evolving compartment syndrome — a limb-threatening emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed recognition of critical ischaemia or compartment syndrome leading to tissue loss.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Sending a weight-bearing patient with an Ottawa-negative inversion injury to ED for an x-ray, generating unnecessary cost, radiation and after-hours ED crowding when clinical clearance is sufficient.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring on reassurance despite a red flag
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being so confident it is 'just a sprain' that inability to weight-bear or bony point tenderness is dismissed, missing an occult fracture.
Recommending a cast, a walking boot, or strict non-weight-bearing for a stable Grade 1 sprain — this slows recovery; protected early mobilisation is correct.
Claiming to have definitively excluded a fracture remotely; the AI can guide an Ottawa self-assessment and risk-stratify, but cannot fully replace palpation and imaging.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Formal palpation for bony point tenderness over the malleoli, base of the fifth metatarsal and navicular
Anterior drawer test for ankle joint stability (mechanical integrity of the ATFL)
Thompson (calf-squeeze) test and formal assessment of active plantarflexion for Achilles integrity
Objective neurovascular examination (pulses, capillary refill, sensation to fine touch)
Plain radiographs of the ankle and foot
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Guide the patient through a structured self-assessment (can they take four steps unaided; self-directed pressing over the bony landmarks; any numbness or a cold/pale foot). If weight-bearing is preserved and there is no bony point tenderness or neurovascular symptom, manage conservatively (RICE, simple analgesia, protected early mobilisation) with clear safety-netting and physiotherapy follow-up. Advise same-day imaging (urgent care / GP) only if the patient cannot bear weight or reports bony point tenderness, and emergency assessment for any neurovascular red flag.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The dominant risk in this case is OVER-action (an unnecessary after-hours ED/x-ray trip for a benign, Ottawa-negative sprain), balanced against the smaller risk of falsely reassuring a patient who in fact has a fracture red flag — hence the explicit weight-bearing and bony-tenderness safety-net.
